note_data.title: Control accounts
keyPoints: A **control account** is a summary account that contains the total of individual accounts in a subsidiary ledger
The **sales ledger control account** summarizes all individual customer accounts and shows total trade receivables
The **purchase ledger control account** summarizes all individual supplier accounts and shows total trade payables
Control accounts are prepared from **totals of books of prime entry**, not individual transactions
Debit entries in sales ledger control include: **credit sales, dishonored cheques, interest charged**
Credit entries in sales ledger control include: **cash received from customers, returns inwards, discounts allowed, bad debts written off**
Debit entries in purchase ledger control include: **cash paid to suppliers, returns outwards, discounts received**
Credit entries in purchase ledger control include: **credit purchases, goods returned by us to suppliers**
Control accounts help **detect errors** by comparing control account balance with total of individual ledger balances
The balance on control account should **equal the total of all individual account balances** in the subsidiary ledger

definitions.definition: A summary account that contains the total of a number of individual accounts from a subsidiary ledger
A control account that summarizes all the individual customer accounts and shows the total amount owed by trade receivables
A control account that summarizes all the individual supplier accounts and shows the total amount owed to trade payables
A ledger containing individual customer or supplier accounts that support the control account

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Write the full control account in T-account format with all entries correctly placed
Show all workings clearly and state the final answer

modelAnswer.question: Explain two benefits of preparing control accounts. (4 marks)
modelAnswer.answer: One benefit is that control accounts help detect errors. This is because the balance on the control account should equal the total of individual ledger balances, so any difference indicates an error. Another benefit is that control accounts save time when preparing financial statements. This is because the balance can be taken directly from the control account rather than adding up all individual balances.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Control accounts help detect errors
Mark 4: Explanation about financial statement preparation

commonMistakes: Never put individual transactions in control accounts — always use totals from books of prime entry
Students often put returns inwards on the debit side — it goes on credit side as it reduces what customers owe
Never include cash sales in sales ledger control — only credit sales appear
Students confuse discounts allowed (credit in sales ledger control) with discounts received (debit in purchase ledger control)
Never include individual customer names in control accounts — they show totals only
examinerTips: Control accounts appear frequently in Paper 2 Section B
Always check that debits equal credits when preparing control accounts
Remember that control account balances appear on the trial balance
Practice identifying whether items are debit or credit entries
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: T-account format showing Sales Ledger Control Account with typical debit entries (opening balance, credit sales, dishonored cheques, interest) and credit entries (cash received, returns inwards, discounts allowed, bad debts, closing balance)
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q3 (8 marks) — they asked students to prepare both sales ledger and purchases ledger control accounts from given transactions including returns, bad debts, and discounts
N/D 2022 P2 Q4 (4 marks) — candidates had to identify which documents are NOT used as sources for control accounts, with many incorrectly choosing bank statements
M/J 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question on advantages of control accounts where segregation of duties was the correct answer
N/D 2023 P2 Q2 (6 marks) — students were required to explain why businesses maintain control accounts as part of double entry bookkeeping system

workedExample.question: Prepare the sales ledger control account for March 2024 from the following information: Opening balance $8,400, Credit sales $15,600, Returns inwards $450, Bad debts written off $200, Cash received from customers $12,800, Discounts allowed $350. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Set up T-account format with 'Sales Ledger Control Account' heading
Step 2: Enter opening balance $8,400 on debit side (money owed by customers)
Step 3: Add credit sales $15,600 on debit side (increases amount owed)
Step 4: Enter returns inwards $450, bad debts $200, cash received $12,800, and discounts allowed $350 on credit side
Step 5: Total both sides and calculate closing balance: $8,400 + $15,600 - $450 - $200 - $12,800 - $350 = $10,200
workedExample.answer: Sales Ledger Control Account: Dr: Opening balance $8,400, Credit sales $15,600, Total $24,000. Cr: Returns inwards $450, Bad debts $200, Cash received $12,800, Discounts allowed $350, Closing balance $10,200, Total $24,000
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct T-account format, 1 mark for opening balance on debit side, 2 marks for all transactions on correct sides, 1 mark for correct totaling, 1 mark for correct closing balance

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused which side transactions should be recorded on' — always remember that increases in amounts owed by customers go on the DEBIT side, while decreases go on CREDIT side
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently included individual customer account details instead of totals' — control accounts only show TOTAL figures, never individual customer amounts
Examiner Report 2024: 'Candidates often forgot to balance off the account properly' — always ensure both sides of the T-account have equal totals and show the closing balance carried down
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a T-account showing Sales Ledger Control Account with debit side containing: opening balance, credit sales, dishonoured cheques; and credit side containing: cash received, returns inwards, discounts allowed, bad debts written off, closing balance. Similarly for Purchases Ledger Control Account but reversed - credit side shows opening balance, credit purchases, and debit side shows cash paid, returns outwards, discounts received, closing balance.
note_data.quickRecap: Control accounts are summary accounts that show total amounts owed by all customers (Sales Ledger Control) or owed to all suppliers (Purchases Ledger Control). They help detect errors by comparing with individual ledger totals and provide internal control through segregation of duties. Sales ledger control increases with sales and decreases with receipts, while purchases ledger control increases with purchases and decreases with payments.
